an5.30:1.2: that on one occasion the Blessed One, on a wandering tour among the Kosalans with a large Saṅgha of monks, arrived at a Kosalan brahman village named Icchānaṅgala.
an5.30:1.3: There he stayed in the Icchānaṅgala forest grove.
an5.30:1.4: The brahman householders of Icchānaṅgala heard it said,
an5.30:1.5: “Gotama the contemplative—the son of the Sakyans, having gone forth from the Sakyan clan—on a wandering tour among the Kosalans with a large Saṅgha of monks—has arrived at Icchānaṅgala and is staying in the Icchānaṅgala forest grove.
an5.30:1.6: And of that Master Gotama this fine reputation has spread:
an5.30:1.7: ‘He is indeed a Blessed One, worthy & rightly self-awakened, consummate in clear-knowing & conduct, well-gone, an expert with regard to the cosmos, unexcelled trainer of people fit to be tamed, teacher of devas & human beings, awakened, blessed.
an5.30:1.8: He makes known—having realized it through direct knowledge—this world with its devas, Māras, & Brahmās, this generation with its contemplatives & brahmans, its royalty & commonfolk;
an5.30:1.9: he explains the Dhamma admirable in the beginning, admirable in the middle, admirable in the end; he expounds the holy life both in its particulars & in its essence, entirely perfect, surpassingly pure.
an5.30:1.10: It is good to see such a worthy one.’”
an5.30:1.11: So the brahman householders of Icchānaṅgala, when the night was gone, taking many staple & non-staple foods, went to the gate house of the Icchānaṅgala forest grove. On arrival they stood there making a loud racket, a great racket.
an5.30:2.1: Now at that time Ven. Nāgita was the Blessed One’s attendant.
an5.30:2.2: So the Blessed One addressed Ven. Nāgita:
an5.30:2.3: “Nāgita, what is that loud racket, that great racket, like fishermen with a catch of fish?”
an5.30:2.4: “Lord, those are the brahman householders of Icchānaṅgala standing at the gate house to the Icchānaṅgala forest grove, having brought many staple & non-staple foods for the sake of the Blessed One & the Saṅgha of monks.”
an5.30:2.5: “May I have nothing to do with honor, Nāgita, and honor nothing to do with me.
an5.30:2.6: Whoever cannot obtain at will—without difficulty, without trouble—as I do, the pleasure of renunciation, the pleasure of seclusion, the pleasure of peace, the pleasure of self-awakening,
an5.30:2.7: let him consent to this slimy-excrement-pleasure, this torpor-pleasure, this pleasure of gains, offerings, & fame.”
an5.30:3.1: “Lord, let the Blessed One acquiesce (to their offerings) now! Let the One Well-Gone acquiesce now!
an5.30:3.2: Now is the time for the Blessed One’s acquiescence, lord! Now is the time for the Blessed One’s acquiescence, lord!
an5.30:3.3: Wherever the Blessed One will go now, the brahmans of the towns & countryside will be so inclined.
an5.30:3.4: Just as when the rain-devas send rain in fat drops, the waters flow with the incline,
an5.30:3.5: in the same way, wherever the Blessed One will go now, the brahmans of the towns & countryside will be so inclined.
an5.30:3.7: Because such is the Blessed One’s virtue & discernment.”
an5.30:4.1: “May I have nothing to do with honor, Nāgita, and honor nothing to do with me.
an5.30:4.2: Whoever cannot obtain at will—without difficulty, without trouble—as I do, the pleasure of renunciation, the pleasure of seclusion, the pleasure of peace, the pleasure of self-awakening,
an5.30:4.3: let him consent to this slimy-excrement-pleasure, this torpor-pleasure, this pleasure of gains, offerings, & fame.
an5.30:4.4: “When one eats & drinks & chews & savors, there is excrement & urine:
an5.30:4.6: “When one loves, there arises the state of change & aberration, sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, & despair:
an5.30:4.8: “When one is committed to the theme of the unattractive, one takes a stance in the loathesomeness of the theme of beauty:
an5.30:4.10: “When one remains focused on the inconstancy of the six media of sensory contact, one takes a stance in the loathsomeness of contact:
an5.30:4.12: “When one remains focused on the arising & passing away of the five clinging-aggregates, one takes a stance in the loathsomeness of clinging:
